speaker: interviewer
text: Ellen, I wonder if you could talk about what it was like to be a German immigrant in the United States following 1939 when the United States was at war with Germany.

speaker: interviewee
text: By 1939, I was no longer in New York. My father did not take kindly to the label of refugee. And we moved. We went to Ohio-- to Toledo, Ohio. I went to Newtown, as I said. And I was the only, quote, 'refugee' in the entire Newtown High School. I became a minor celebrity. I was always on the-- Newtown had its own radio station because it was 10,000 kids. And I became a regular member. That's how good my English was. Within, certainly-- I mean, five months after living, being in America, I spoke English almost the way I speak it today. We moved to Toledo, Ohio. I did-- I was always a good student. I was blessed with, first of all, a built-in computer, and secondly, a photographic memory. And you put those two things together, and school was very easy. And we moved to Toledo, Ohio. And I went to college there. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] It-- there-- I was the only-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I think I was the only German, quote, 'refugee' there. And besides that, my English was so good. And I wanted to be an actress. I decided to be an actress when I was five. My father didn't look at me the first three days of my life because I was a girl. And he decided as long as I was a girl and he wouldn't have a boy that the least I could do was to be the second Madame Curie. And so I studied chemistry and math in college to please my father. And it came very easily to me. At the end of-- it-- I was like any other American kid. You would not have known. I appeared in school plays. I did Shakespeare. The only mistake that I made is when I did Olivia in And instead of saying, 'I vow,' I said, 'I wowed them' because V and Ws are very different in English and German. But I was your typical American kid. After college, I came to New York. And I became an actress. And I did 250 television commercials as the typical American mom.

speaker: interviewee
text: So that-- I didn't want to remember Germany. And I didn't want to be a German refugee. I wanted to assimilate as quickly as possible. I wanted to be like everybody else.

speaker: interviewee
text: I mean, teenagers don't want to be different. They want to be the same as their peers. So I-- I didn't stick out. No-- very few people-- I-- Before I came to New York, I went to the Cleveland Play House. I became Luther Adler's protege under one condition, that no one-- he didn't know that I was born in Germany. I never told him. He made me promise. He said, I will open doors for you in New York under one condition-- that you never let anyone know that you're Jewish. Now, I never denied the fact that I was Jewish. People just never asked. And I was fortunate. I, I-- it took a long time for me to admit to my friends that I had been born in Germany. I didn't want to be German. I wanted to forget the whole thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: And can you just briefly tell us what course your career took in terms of the media you worked in and--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I came to New York. I went to work in radio, early television, stage. I starred-- in a flop-- a Broadway flop. I was taken to Hollywood for my screen test. But I had just met the man that I later married. I married a Broadway producer. And he didn't want an actress as a wife. He did allow me to do those 250 television commercials because we needed the money. And-- even though in those days, they didn't pay like they do today. And I-- he talked me into helping him. And I worked with him producing. We sent shows on the road. And we traveled all over the country to visit our shows. And after he died of a brain tumor very early on, on our 13th wedding anniversary. And last week was the 33rd anniversary of his death. I have two children. They were small children when he died. And after his death, I went on producing. And I came to Los Angeles the end of '75. I've been here for 21 years because my son decided at the age of three. My father-in-law was an exhibitor. At one time, Brandt Theaters was the largest chain of motion pictures in America. He had a chain of 150 theaters-- independent chain, not to be confused with Loews and RKO. But he had the largest chain of independent motion picture theaters in America. My husband was a Broadway producer. My son decided he did not want to follow in either dad's or grandfather's footsteps. And he is now a literary agent here. But he always wanted to make movies. He had misspent his youth in front of the tube. And he always wanted to be in Hollywood.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's back up just a moment. What was your husband's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My husband's name was George Brandt.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your stage name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My stage name was Ellen Fenwick.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your--

speaker: interviewee
text: Friedsam was much too difficult a name. And I got it out of the phone book-- the Detroit phone book. The Toledo phone book, where we lived, wasn't big enough. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is your daughter's name? And what is your son's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my daughter's name is Jody. She was born Jody Lauren Brandt. And she now is Jody Grotzinger. And she has a son, a 14-year-old son. His name is Derek-- Derek Harrison Grotzinger. And my son's name is Geoffrey-- Geoffrey Mark Brandt. He is named after Lord Jeffery Amherst because my husband graduated high school at the age of 12 or 13-- I don't know exactly which. And he decided not to go to college because nobody could teach him anything. He knew more than all the teachers, which is how you feel when you graduate high school at 13. And-- but he did visit his brother, who was at Amherst as an undergraduate. And he decided that he would have a son someday. And his son, at least, would make up for him. He felt sorry then. In his 20s, he became smart. And he decided that his son would go to Amherst. And so my son's name is Geoffrey-- G-E-O-- Geoffrey Mark Brandt. And he has a daughter. And her name is Taylor Brandt.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you think about your children and your grandchildren, what is it that you want them to be sure to know about you and your life?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would love them-- I have written a book about the period of 1933 to 1938, which unfortunately, I've never been able to have published. I-- children of-- whether they're survivors or escapees-- I'm an escapee. I never went to a concentration camp, thank god. Children of escapees and survivors fall into two very distinct categories-- the ones who want to know every last detail and go and look at concentration camps where their parents were incarcerated and become dedicated for the rest of their lives; and children who want to know nothing about Germany, who want their parents to be like other parents and don't want their parents to be different. Unfortunately, my children fall into that category, except for my daughter, who has a relationship, a longstanding relationship, with a very lovely man who is much more Jewish than any of the rest of us. And she has reverted back to Judaism. And I went to Germany with her last year-- to  Berlin. I had always been too proud to accept the invitation that is extended to all former Berlin residents by the mayor of Berlin to come to Berlin for a week. And when I make contact with my friends from the Goldschmidt Schule, they all said, you must go. They really do a good job. And they really in-- for a whole week, they try to apologize what they've done to us. And I said, I don't need them. I can go on my own. I've been there once. And I can go again. And they all said, no, it's not the same.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I went last year, and my daughter came with me. And she learned a little bit about my life. She met my girlfriend who still lives in the building where we lived, for instance.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I ran a-- I found that I could do a simultaneous running commentary from German to English. I'm still totally, totally bilingual. And I made a perfect interpreter for her.

speaker: interviewee
text: So that-- I would love my children to know a little bit about-- a little more than they know about my life in Germany. And--

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm sorry that it took me so long to try and find my roots-- that I had to get old until I became Jewish and German again. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you see as the result in your life now of your life in Germany, those 16 years?

speaker: interviewee
text: The 16 years probably held me back for the rest of my life. I-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] it's a miracle I've never landed in jail because, to this day, I react violently to uniforms, [LAUGHS] whether they are policemen or doormen. [LAUGHS] No matter what, I hate anything in a uniform. As I said, I still, to this day, cannot go even to a bar mitzvah in a temple. It, it brings back nightmares. I had nightmares for years until, until after I was married. It took a couple of years of a wonderful, wonderful marriage to really give me inner peace. I think my life would have been a totally different life without those 16 years-- absolutely.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is it something that you think about often, or is it--

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: --something that comes up--

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: --at times when you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --least expect it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, only at times when I least expect it. I-- people have asked me over and over again whether I feel guilty that I escaped. And I've talked about this to every German that I have met. No, I have never experienced guilt for one second. I am sorry. I'm so sorry for all the people who died, who didn't have a life. I'm glad that I escaped. I don't feel guilt.

speaker: interviewee
text: I do feel that I owe it to the six million who perished to watch and to put myself in, in harm's way and to watch, for instance, and to expose myself to this-- to, to do-- to have the nightmares afterwards, you know? To do-- have a little bit of a guilt trip. To have felt guilty? No. Is there anything else that you would like to add to what you've said as we finish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not really. I think I told you the other day about the statistics. But whether they are important-- I mean, I would like to add one thing, yes, a general thing. And that is, unfortunately, I just talked about going to see films that I feel that I should see. With the exception of and every other film was made by Americans for Americans. And they are perpetuating misconceptions, which is too bad. Because, in general, Americans, to this day, really don't know what went on. Americans, to this day, have no idea of the early warning signs that we had, that it could happen in America, that percentage of Jews in America is shrinking with every year, that the percentage of Jews in Germany is 1% of the general population. There were only 600,000 Jews in Germany out of a population of 60 million. 150 of those perished in the Holocaust. 450,000 of us got out alive because we had the early warning sign. I would love Americans to pay more attention to the similarities of Germany in the '30s-- Germany in 1928, when there was no anti-Semitism, and my father didn't leave. America today, where there is no anti-Semitism-- we think. It's there. It's always there. And I would like Americans to be more aware of the tremendous similarities of Germany then and America today.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ellen, thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome. It's a pleasure. Thank you. This is the coat of arms. The words-- the legend-- reads, 'Nihil paenitere,' which means 'Never regret.'

speaker: interviewer
text: And which coat  of arms is this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a bourgeois-- one of 250 bourgeois coats of arms awarded to Germans. It was awarded about 1750.

speaker: interviewer
text: And again, the family name is?

speaker: interviewee
text: Friedsam-- 'peaceful.' This is the official wedding photo of my mother and father. They were married in April of 1918, while the war was still going on. My father is in his uniform.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what are their names, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Guido Andreas Friedsam. And her maiden name was Tillie Behr.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was this photo taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: And this was taken in Landau Pfalz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Germany.

speaker: interviewee
text: Germany. On the far-- well, is it--

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a photograph of Landhaustrasse 44, where we lived in Berlin. We lived on the second floor on the left-hand side. Each apartment ended in a balcony. The picture was taken in 1933 when we moved in. And this is a photograph of the same building. And it features the balcony, the second-floor balcony, which was important in my life.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1969. I've been back to Berlin twice, once in '69. And this was the trip last year, when I went with my daughter-- 1994, the same building. 1995-- I beg your pardon. This is a document attesting, once again, that my father had won the Iron Cross-- one, two, three, and so on. The interesting thing about this document is that it is from the Fuhrer and the Vice Chancellor. And it is dated 1935 and says specifically that my father won the Iron Crosses during World War I. This is a-- the affidavit, dated January 1938. That's the thing that gave us life.

speaker: interviewer
text: And it was from what country?

speaker: interviewee
text: From America-- to enter America. This is a, a picture of my parents' 40th wedding anniversary in 1958 in New York. From the top-- my husband; naturally, our dog; and myself; then my dad and my mother; and my two small children, Geoffrey and Jody.

speaker: interviewer
text: And would you also begin with your husband's name and then go through them?

speaker: interviewee
text: George Brandt, Muffin, Ellen Brandt, Guido Friedsam, Tillie Friedsam, Geoffrey Mark Brandt, and Jody Lauren Brandt. This is a picture of my mother on her 90th birthday. My mother lived-- she died four weeks shy of her 100th birthday. This was taken in Los Angeles.

speaker: interviewer
text: And her name, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: And her name was Tillie Friedsam.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what year was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1982. This is my oldest grandchild, my daughter's son, Derek-- Derek Harrison Grotzinger, age 14.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was it taken and when was it--

speaker: interviewee
text: Taken in Los Angeles.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you know when?

speaker: interviewee
text: Last year.

speaker: interviewer
text: 19-- what year?

speaker: interviewee
text: Two years ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what year would that have been?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1994. This is a picture of my other grandchild, my granddaughter, Taylor [? Gordon ?] Brandt, in all her Aunt Jody's finery. Picture was taken in Los Angeles in the summer of 1994. She's now 4 and 1/2.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ellen, do you have anything that you'd like to say?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I would like to introduce my two children. This is my son, Geoffrey Mark Brandt, and my daughter, Jody Grotzinger. I would like to add something to a question that you asked me earlier. I am currently writing for my grandchildren-- because my grandson asked me-- I am writing a family history. And I would be very grateful to both of my children if they would, when I'm not here anymore and their children are grown up, that they would tell them a little bit about my experiences in Germany. I would like to keep the memory alive. Thank you. This is my son, Geoffrey Mark Brandt, who is a literary agent in Los Angeles, and my daughter, Jody Grotzinger-- Jody Brandt Grotzinger-- who also lives in Los Angeles and works for Bravo.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do either of you have anything you might like to say before we end this tape?

speaker: interviewee
text: I do. I am fully an American. I grew up as an American child. I was raised in an environment where I did not speak German. And yet I grew up with a sense of what it was like to come from a family that had come from Germany. Probably my favorite food in the whole world are German Christmas cookies, which we have every year at Christmastime. And I do have a sense of my cultural--

speaker: interviewee
text: Hanukkah time.

speaker: interviewee
text: Hanukkah time.

speaker: interviewee
text: Hanukkah time.

speaker: interviewee
text: Christmastime.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: They were Christmas cookies. They were German Christmas cookies. And I do have a sense of what it was like to be the child of a survivor of that era. And it's, I think, affected who we all are. So it hasn't all been in vain.

speaker: interviewee
text: Good.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we will tell our children and their children's children about your background.

speaker: interviewee
text: Good. I would like to perpetuate the memory and not have it be forgotten. And hopefully, it will not happen again.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.
